# Drive main deployments from successful releases

Main-branch deployments are driven by a single Release Plan produced after all application and package Releases for the change succeed. This delays deployment by the release workflow and couples delivery to release availability, but prevents unpublished code from reaching an environment, provides a dependency-aware basis for selective deployment, and avoids starting one competing deployment for every GitHub Release created by a merge; Preview Environments continue to use change detection because pull requests do not produce Releases. Release analysis remains exhaustive across the monorepo so dependency version propagation has one source of truth, while the release tool publishes only workspaces that require a Release and deployment selects only the resulting Affected Deployment Units. A change to deployable files that produces no corresponding Release fails explicitly instead of being treated as an empty plan. The plan maps each Deployment Unit to the exact Release tag of its owning workspace, and both Develop and production build that unit from the tag using its existing commands so their shared units remain release-aligned; a package tag cannot stand in for an application tag when the unit deploys an application, and a selected unit without its own corresponding tag fails planning. Releases do not contain deployment artifacts. Manual full deployment accepts one successfully released main-branch commit as its boundary and derives the latest owning-workspace tag for every unit reachable from that commit, using the same tag map for Develop and production rather than permitting arbitrary version combinations; it cannot bypass the release requirement.
